intro:
Judgment (Hebrew mishpat, "justice, verdict, the right ordering of things"; Greek krisis and krima, "a separating, a sentence") is God's righteous governance of the moral order he has made — his discernment of right from wrong and his rendering to each what is due. Because "the Judge of all the earth" cannot but "do what is just" (Genesis 18:25), judgment is not an arbitrary intrusion but the outworking of his holiness and faithfulness. He "judges the world with righteousness" and the peoples with his truth (Psalm 96:13; Romans 2:2), so that his verdicts are impartial, searching the heart and not the outward appearance (1 Samuel 16:7; Jeremiah 17:10).

In the Old Testament judgment unfolds first as temporal acts within history: the Flood (Genesis 6:5–7), the scattering at Babel, the overthrow of Sodom and Gomorrah (Genesis 19:24–25), the plagues on Egypt, and ultimately the exile of Israel and the fall of Jerusalem — each a specific verdict against sin. Yet mishpat also means deliverance: God "judges" the oppressed by vindicating them, and the prophets long for the day when justice will "roll down like waters" (Amos 5:24). These threads gather into the Day of the LORD, a coming reckoning that is darkness for the wicked and salvation for the faithful (Joel 2:1–2; Zephaniah 1:14–18; Malachi 4:1–2), and into Daniel's vision of thrones set and books opened (Daniel 7:9–10).

The New Testament intensifies this hope into a final, universal judgment entrusted to the Son. The Father "has given all judgment to the Son" and authority to execute it because he is the Son of Man (John 5:22, 27); God "has fixed a day on which he will judge the world in righteousness by a man whom he has appointed," certifying this by raising him from the dead (Acts 17:31). Christ portrays it as the separation of sheep from goats (Matthew 25:31–46). Every person — believer and unbeliever alike — must appear before the judgment seat to give account (Romans 14:10; 2 Corinthians 5:10; Hebrews 9:27), and at the great white throne the dead are judged "according to what they had done" as written in the books (Revelation 20:11–15).

The gospel's astonishing claim is that the Judge has borne the judgment. At the cross God's justice against sin and his vindication of the righteous converge: condemnation falls on Christ so that "there is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus" (Romans 8:1). Whoever believes "does not come into judgment, but has passed from death to life" (John 5:24; John 3:18). Judgment therefore stands as both warning and comfort: it assures the oppressed that evil will not have the last word, summons all people to repentance, and grounds the believer's hope — for the One who will judge the world is the same Lord who was judged in their place. "Shall not the Judge of all the earth do what is just?" remains Scripture's settled confidence.
